html:
Magic worked on Muhammad — he hallucinated doing things he had not done Prophetic Character Logical Inconsistency Strong Bukhari 3043
"Once the Prophet was bewitched so that he began to imagine that he had done a thing which in fact he had not done."

What the hadith says

A Jewish man named Labid bin Al-A'sam performed magic on Muhammad using hair and palm material placed in a well. Muhammad began hallucinating — believing he had done things he had not done. The magic operated for months before being discovered and neutralized through revelation of Surahs 113 and 114.

Why this is a problem

  1. A prophet's mental states were proven unreliable. If an ordinary human could plant false memories in Muhammad using hair and a comb, the claim that his experiences of revelation, angels, and paradise were veridical cannot be verified. The hadith establishes that his inner states could be false.
  2. The Quran denies this happened. Q 17:47 calls "a man bewitched" a false accusation leveled by disbelievers — yet the hadith confirms he actually was bewitched. The tradition preserves both claims simultaneously without resolving the contradiction.

Why it fails

The "worldly but not prophetic" distinction is a modern theological patch not present in the hadith. If a sorcerer could plant false memories in Muhammad for months, the claim that none of his revelation was affected during that period cannot be verified — it is stipulated by the same tradition that documents the vulnerability. Q 5:67's promise that Allah will "protect you from the people" is directly undermined by a Jewish man with a comb achieving exactly what that promise was meant to prevent.

"I have been made victorious with terror cast in the hearts of the enemy" Treatment of Disbelievers Prophetic Character Strong Bukhari 2855
"Allah's Apostle said, 'I have been sent with the shortest expressions bearing the widest meanings, and I have been made victorious with terror (cast in the hearts of the enemy), and while I was sleeping, the keys of the treasures of the world were brought to me and put in my hand.'"

What the hadith says

Muhammad lists three divine privileges unique to him. The second: he was made victorious through ru'b — terror cast into his enemies' hearts. This is Muhammad's own description of the mechanism behind his military success.

Why this is a problem

"Made victorious with terror" is Muhammad's biographical self-report about method, not metaphor. It matches Q 8:12 ("I will cast terror into the hearts of disbelievers") and Q 8:60 ("terrify the enemy of Allah"). The Arabic ru'b is the root of modern Arabic irhab — terrorism. The claim that Islam has nothing to do with terror as a tool is difficult to sustain when the Prophet boasts of being uniquely equipped with it as a divine military advantage.

Why it fails

Whether the terror was supernatural or tactical, the Prophet's biography credits it as the source of victory. Classical military doctrine (al-Mawardi, al-Shaybani) developed the Quranic and hadith terror-language into active principles of projecting fear, including exemplary executions. The modern jihadist citation of this hadith is not a misreading — it is application of a tradition classical jurisprudence developed systematically.

Banu Qurayza: Sa'd rules to kill all men, enslave women and children — Muhammad calls it Allah's judgment Prophetic Character Treatment of Disbelievers Strong Bukhari 2918
"Sad said, 'I give the judgment that their warriors should be killed and their children and women should be taken as prisoners.' The Prophet then remarked, 'O Sad! You have judged amongst them with (or similar to) the judgment of the King Allah.'"

What the hadith says

After the Banu Qurayza surrendered following the Battle of the Trench, Sa'd ibn Mu'adh ruled: execute all adult men, enslave the women and children. Muhammad endorsed this as matching Allah's own judgment. Between 600 and 900 men were beheaded in the Medina marketplace in a single day.

Why this is a problem

  1. Muhammad explicitly endorses mass execution as divine will. "The judgment of Allah the King" is Muhammad's own phrase. The killing is not merely permitted — it is theologically credited as what Allah himself would have ruled.
  2. Collective punishment was applied without establishing individual guilt. The tribe's alleged treaty breach was assigned collectively; all adult males paid with their lives. Their families were enslaved. The hadith's moral spotlight is on Sa'd's "good judgment" — the mass killing is the unremarkable backdrop.

Why it fails

The Deuteronomic rule applied to cities that refused peace before siege, not surrendered internal allies. More importantly, Muhammad chose Sa'd specifically and endorsed the verdict as "Allah's judgment" — making the prophetic authorization explicit and the "not Muhammad's initiative" framing untenable. The Quran endorses the outcome (Q 33:26–27). A mass execution of surrendered prisoners, divinely ratified, is not improved by rewriting its legal framework.

"If somebody discards his religion, kill him" Treatment of Disbelievers Strong Bukhari 2895
"Ali burnt some people and this news reached Ibn 'Abbas, who said, 'Had I been in his place I would not have burnt them... No doubt, I would have killed them, for the Prophet said, "If somebody (a Muslim) discards his religion, kill him."'"

What the hadith says

Ali burned a group of apostates. Ibn Abbas criticized the method — burning is reserved for Allah — but affirmed the principle: apostates must be killed. He quotes Muhammad directly: "If somebody discards his religion, kill him."

Why this is a problem

This is the hadith foundation of the apostasy death penalty. It is a direct statement attributed to Muhammad, preserved in Bukhari, acted on by Ali, and affirmed by Ibn Abbas. All four major Sunni legal schools prescribe death for male apostates on this basis. Saudi Arabia, Iran, Mauritania, Afghanistan, and Qatar still carry apostasy death penalties in law. Q 2:256 ("no compulsion in religion") cannot coherently coexist with "kill apostates" — classical scholarship resolved the tension by treating Q 2:256 as abrogated.

Why it fails

The classical consensus treated apostasy itself as capital without requiring additional hostile acts. The command — "if somebody discards his religion, kill him" — attaches to the religious act of leaving, not to any violent supplement. Six canonical collections preserve the command. Current enforcement in Saudi Arabia, Iran, and Mauritania applies to private belief change. "No compulsion" and "death for leaving" cannot both be operative — and the tradition's abrogation doctrine resolved the tension by dropping "no compulsion."

Women are "deficient in intelligence and religion" — most of Hell is women Women Strong Bukhari 301
"[Muhammad] said: 'O women! Give alms, for I have seen that the majority of the dwellers of Hell-Fire were you (women).'... He replied, 'O women! You curse frequently, and are ungrateful to your husbands. I have not seen anyone more deficient in intelligence and religion than you.'"

What the hadith says

Muhammad tells women that: (1) most of Hell's inhabitants are women; (2) women are deficient in intelligence and religion (naqisat 'aql wa din). He defines the deficiencies explicitly: intellectual deficiency because two women equal one male witness (Q 2:282); religious deficiency because women cannot pray during menstruation.

Why this is a problem

The reasoning is circular. The Quran requires two female witnesses to equal one male; therefore women are declared intellectually deficient; therefore the rule is justified. The rule is used as evidence for the generalization that presumably justified the rule. Similarly, Islamic law exempts women from prayer during menstruation; the hadith then declares women religiously deficient because they do not pray during menstruation — condemning them for obeying a command they were given.

This hadith is cited in classical fiqh to bar women from judgeship, justify male guardianship, and restrict women's testimony. It remains active in modern Muslim popular discourse on women's "emotional nature."

Why it fails

The hadith's language is categorical: "I have not seen anyone more deficient in intelligence and religion than you" — addressed to "O women" as a class. Classical jurisprudence did not treat it as pastoral context; it built juridical rules on it. A tradition whose founder declares half of humanity intellectually and religiously deficient, and whose legal superstructure embeds those declarations as operative doctrine, cannot claim the statement was situational encouragement.

Jesus returns to break crosses, kill pigs, and abolish the jizya — ending Christianity Jesus / Christology Treatment of Disbelievers Strong Bukhari 2380
"Allah's Apostle said, 'The Hour will not be established until the son of Mary (i.e. Jesus) descends amongst you as a just ruler, he will break the cross, kill the pigs, and abolish the Jizya tax...'"

What the hadith says

At the end of time, Jesus returns physically. He will: (1) break crosses — destroy Christianity's central symbol; (2) kill pigs — eliminate the animal associated with Christian diet; (3) abolish the jizya — the tax that permitted non-Muslims to continue practicing their faith under Islamic governance. With no jizya, there is no legal accommodation for continued non-Muslim existence.

Why this is a problem

The abolition of the jizya is the structural core of the problem. Under classical Islamic law, non-Muslims survived under Muslim rule by paying this tax. Abolishing it eliminates that option — any surviving Christian at Jesus's return must either convert or face the alternative. This is mainstream Sunni eschatology, preserved in classical commentary without softening: the Islamic vision of history's end is the end of Christianity as a distinct tradition.

Why it fails

"Rectification" means the messiah Christians worship returns to dismantle their religion's symbols and collapse the legal framework permitting their continued existence as non-Muslims. That is eschatological supersessionism, not reconciliation. A prophecy in which one faith's messiah returns to eliminate another faith's legal standing is not a vision of pluralism, regardless of the theological framing.

Muhammad married Safiya the same day he killed her husband and family at Khaybar Prophetic Character Women Treatment of Disbelievers Strong Bukhari 367
"Dihya came and said, 'O Allah's Prophet! Give me a slave girl from the captives.' The Prophet said, 'Go and take any slave girl.' He took Safiya bint Huyai. A man came to the Prophet and said, '...she befits none but you.' So the Prophet said, 'Bring him along with her.'... The Prophet then manumitted her and married her..."

What the hadith says

At Khaybar, Muhammad's forces killed the Jewish tribe's men. Safiya bint Huyai — a 17-year-old whose husband was executed that day — was taken as a slave. Muhammad claimed her, formally freed her, and married her the same night. Her freedom was declared her marriage dower.

Why this is a problem

  1. The sequence: morning — kill her husband; evening — marry her. Whatever theological framework is applied, the same man who ordered her husband's execution consummated a "marriage" with her hours later. She had no family, no community, no power, no alternative.
  2. "Freedom as dower" is not consent. Declaring someone free and naming that freedom as the bride-price is not a gift — it is a transaction in which the enslaved woman's release is leveraged as compensation for the marriage itself.

Why it fails

A woman who has watched her husband and family killed, who has been taken as a slave, and who faces the choice between remaining with her captor as a "wife" or facing the alternative has no free choice in any meaningful sense. "He could have kept her as a slave but married her instead" is not a defense — it is a description of which form of coercive control was chosen.

Angels curse a wife until morning if she refuses her husband's sexual advance Women Strong Bukhari 3104
"Allah's Apostle said, 'If a husband calls his wife to his bed (i.e. to have sexual relation) and she refuses and causes him to sleep in anger, the angels will curse her till morning.'"

What the hadith says

If a wife refuses her husband's sexual advance and he goes to sleep angry, Allah's angels actively curse her throughout the night. No exceptions or qualifications are given.

Why this is a problem

  1. The wife's consent is irrelevant to the framework. Her refusal — for any reason — triggers supernatural punishment. Illness, exhaustion, or simply not wanting to: none appear in the text as legitimate grounds for refusal.
  2. The asymmetry is total. No comparable curse falls on a husband who refuses his wife. The obligation is one-directional, enforced by divine cursing authority, with the standard for legitimacy being the husband's emotional state, not the wife's condition.

Why it fails

The "legitimate reasons" qualification is juristic elaboration; it is not in the hadith. Classical jurisprudence derived from this hadith the doctrine of tamkeen — sexual access as an enforceable husbandly right — which in classical formulations effectively removes the wife's consent from the marital relation. A cosmos whose angels curse a woman for saying no has sanctified marital coercion regardless of how later jurisprudence softened the enforcement mechanism.

"Don't beat your wife like a stallion camel and then sleep with her the same night" Women Prophetic Character Strong Bukhari 5813
"The Prophet forbade laughing at a person who passes wind, and said, 'How does anyone of you beat his wife as he beats the stallion camel and then he may embrace (sleep with) her?' And Hisham said, 'As he beats his slave.'"

What the hadith says

Muhammad criticized men who beat their wives with the same ferocity used on male camels and then had sex with them the same night. A sub-narrator substitutes "slave" for "wife," showing the two were grammatically interchangeable in the original context.

Why this is a problem

Read carefully, this is not a prohibition of wife-beating. The hadith does not say "don't beat your wife." It says don't beat her like a stallion camel — with that specific level of brute force. Some beating is assumed permissible; only its severity is questioned. This aligns with Q 4:34, which instructs husbands to "strike" rebellious wives. The hadith shows the classical understanding: beating is permitted within limits — not so severe that sleeping with her afterward becomes unseemly.

Why it fails

The hadith does not say "don't beat your wife" — it says "don't beat her like a stallion camel." A prohibition on one degree of severity preserves the category while adjusting intensity. If Muhammad intended to forbid wife-beating entirely, he could have said so directly — as he did for other acts elsewhere in the corpus. The camel-severity limit is the actual content of the teaching.

"I have been ordered to fight the people until they testify and offer prayer and pay zakat" Treatment of Disbelievers Prophetic Character Strong Bukhari 25
"Allah's Apostle said: 'I have been ordered (by Allah) to fight against the people until they testify that none has the right to be worshipped but Allah and that Muhammad is Allah's Apostle, and offer the prayers perfectly and give the obligatory charity, so if they perform that, then they save their lives and property from me...'"

What the hadith says

Muhammad states he was divinely commanded to fight all people until they accept Islam — specifically until they profess the shahada, pray, and pay zakat. Only conversion to Islam purchases safety from him.

Why this is a problem

This hadith appears in both Bukhari and Muslim — the two most authoritative Sunni collections. It directly contradicts Q 2:256 ("no compulsion in religion") while matching Q 9:5 and Q 9:29. Classical Islamic law — the doctrines of dar al-harb and offensive jihad — was built on this hadith. When modern Muslim apologists claim "Islam doesn't force conversion," they are contradicting the Prophet's own stated mission.

Why it fails

The hadith's language is first-person and universal: "I have been ordered to fight the people." Classical scholarship built entire juridical systems on this universality — the offensive jihad doctrine, the division of the world into dar al-Islam and dar al-harb, the permissibility of initiating war against non-Muslim polities. Retroactively contextualizing the hadith as emergency-specific requires discarding 1,300 years of jurisprudence built on it.

No Muslim shall be executed for killing a disbeliever — life value is tiered by religion Treatment of Disbelievers Logical Inconsistency Strong Bukhari 111
"'Ali replied, '...the law that no Muslim should be killed in Qisas (equality in punishment) for the killing of a disbeliever.'"

What the hadith says

Ali records a piece of written Prophetic law: the principle of qisas — equal retribution — does not apply across the Muslim/non-Muslim line. A Muslim who kills a non-Muslim is not subject to the capital punishment available when a Muslim kills another Muslim.

Why this is a problem

This is the foundational hadith for one of the most consequential inequalities in classical Islamic law. The diyya (blood money) for a killed non-Muslim is typically half or a third of that owed for a killed Muslim. A Muslim murderer of a Christian or Jew is not subject to the death penalty applicable when a Muslim murders another Muslim. This is not archaic — Saudi Arabia has historically applied differential diyya by religion, and the principle persists in several Muslim-majority criminal codes today.

Why it fails

The text does not limit the rule to a specific political arrangement. It states a legal principle: Muslims are not killed for killing disbelievers. Classical law operationalized this as permanent doctrine. Modern reformist readings require either rejecting the hadith or arguing that "Islamic justice" means something fundamentally different from what fourteen centuries of jurisprudence held it to mean.

"Best of peoples" — because you bring them in chains until they embrace Islam Treatment of Disbelievers Strong Bukhari 4351
"The Verse: 'You (true Muslims) are the best of peoples ever raised up for mankind' (3:110) means, the best of peoples for the people, as you bring them with chains on their necks till they embrace Islam."

What the hadith says

Abu Huraira's authoritative interpretation of Q 3:110 — "the best nation" — is that Muslims are the best of peoples because they bring others in chains until those people accept Islam. The virtue of the best nation consists in its success at coercive conversion.

Why this is a problem

This is not a modern extremist reading. It is companion-level Quranic exegesis from the most prolific hadith narrator in Islam, preserved in Bukhari. The "best nation" verse is one of Islam's most cited self-descriptions. Its classical interpretation explicitly ties Muslim superiority to bringing non-Muslims to Islam in chains. Ibn Kathir, Tabari, and other major classical commentators take it literally — war captives chained, marched toward conversion. The historical practice of conquest, enslavement, and conversion-for-release is the referent.

Why it fails

The classical tafsir tradition (Tabari, Ibn Kathir) read the image literally as captives brought toward conversion — consistent with the actual historical practice of conquest-plus-conversion the early Muslim community conducted. The "firm instruction" reading emerged as the practice became politically inconvenient to defend. The framing of Muslim superiority as the mission of bringing others in chains is not incidental rhetoric — it is the exegetical logic by which conquest-plus-conversion was theologically meritorious in the classical tradition.
